one_line_takeaway: An extended-abstract announcement of PAN 2026's five shared tasks (AI-text detection, text watermarking, multi-author style analysis, generative plagiarism detection, and reasoning-trajectory source/safety detection) with redesigned datasets and evaluation setups, but no experiments or results of its own.
key_finding: There are no empirical findings, since this is a prospective task-announcement paper, not a results paper. The paper's substantive claims are retrospective/organizational: in 2025 the four PAN tasks that ran received 112 software submissions and 70 notebook papers combined; since PAN's founding in 2007 it has organized 82 shared tasks; since 2012, more than 1,100 submissions have been made via TIRA. The authors also state a critical self-assessment of the prior Generative Plagiarism Detection task design (2025 edition): paragraph-substitution plagiarism was 'relatively easy to detect... using simple embedding similarity calculations,' motivating the from-scratch redesign for 2026.
core_contribution: This is a shared-task overview / extended abstract announcing the PAN 2026 lab (a CLEF-affiliated workshop on stylometry and text forensics). It is not an empirical research paper: it presents no experiments, models, or results of its own. Its contribution is the design and announcement of five shared tasks to be run in 2026, three continuing prior PAN tasks with new datasets and two entirely new: (1) Voight-Kampff Generative AI Detection (continuing the 2024/2025 task, run jointly with the ELOQUENT lab in a builder-breaker setup where ELOQUENT participants craft obfuscated texts to break PAN participants' AI-text detectors); (2) Text Watermarking, a new task where participants embed a watermark into an existing (not necessarily LLM-generated) text and later verify it after the text passes through unknown automated obfuscation steps, executed as a two-step stateless-sandbox pipeline on the TIRA platform to prevent information leakage between the embedding and verification stages; (3) Multi-Author Writing Style Analysis, continuing the task of locating writing-style/authorship change points, now using a new dataset built from fanfiction text mixed via semantic and stylistic representations into three sub-datasets of differing topical/stylistic similarity; (4) Generative Plagiarism Detection, redesigned from the 2025 edition (which inserted plagiarized paragraphs directly and was described by the authors as unrealistic and too easy to catch via embedding similarity) to instead generate plagiarism from source documents from scratch, covering merging (summarizing multiple sources) and expanding (splitting one source into multiple plagiarized paragraphs) plagiarism, with new domains from PubMed and JSTOR, split into a Source Retrieval subtask and a Text Alignment subtask; (5) Reasoning Trajectory Detection, a new task with a Source Detection subtask (classify whether a reasoning trajectory + final answer, given a user query, was produced by an AI system or a human, across math/coding/financial-reasoning queries) and a Safety Detection subtask (given a query from one of three categories -- risky queries requesting harmful content, jailbreak attacks with obscured risk, and benign queries containing risky tokens -- classify whether the reasoning trajectory and the final answer are each safe or unsafe).
threat_model: The paper itself defines no unified adversarial threat model (it is a lab/workshop announcement, not an attack or defense paper). The closest analogues, described only as task designs rather than as the authors' own attacks or defenses: for Task 1, an adversary applies unknown/adversarial text obfuscations to AI-generated text to evade detection, and PAN/ELOQUENT jointly play builder (detector) vs. breaker (obfuscator) roles; for Task 2, an adversary applies unknown automated text-modification/obfuscation processes intended to destroy an embedded watermark, and the submitted system's watermark must survive while remaining inconspicuous; for Task 5's Safety Detection subtask, the query-side adversary issues jailbreak attacks that obscure harmful intent or crafts benign-looking queries containing risky tokens, and the detection system must classify the resulting reasoning trajectory and final answer as safe or unsafe at both the step level and the outcome level. These are threat models for future shared-task participants to address, not threat models the authors themselves evaluate against a system in this paper.
limitations: As a task-announcement/extended-abstract paper, it inherently reports no validated results, baselines, or ablations for any of the five tasks -- everything described (datasets, difficulty, robustness of proposed methods) is a plan, not a measured outcome, so none of it can be taken as evidence of what will actually work. The paper does not specify size, language coverage, or exact composition of the new datasets (fanfiction corpus, PubMed/JSTOR plagiarism corpus, reasoning-trajectory corpus) beyond source and construction method. It does not specify evaluation metrics for any task in this extended abstract. The Multilingual Text Detoxification task, run in 2025, is explicitly dropped for 2026 pending further analysis, and the human-AI collaboration-degree subtask from 2025 is also dropped, without giving quantitative reasons in this text beyond wanting to 'take time analyzing the lessons learned.'

quantitative_results: PAN 2025 (prior year's four tasks, not this year's): 112 software submissions and 70 notebook papers
Since PAN's inception (2007) through 2026: 82 shared tasks organized
Since 2012: more than 1,100 submissions made via the TIRA platform
